elements.sent-2411-001: Congestion on highways has become a significant social problem due to the increasing number of vehicles, leading to considerable waste of time and pollution.
elements.sent-2411-002: Regulating the outflow from the \acrlong{ST can help alleviate this congestion.}
elements.sent-2411-003: Notably, traffic flows follow recurring patterns over days and weeks, allowing for the application of \acrfull{ILC .}
elements.sent-2411-004: Building on these insights, we propose an \acrshort{ILC approach based on the Cell Transmission Model with service stations (CTM-s).}
elements.sent-2411-005: It is shown that \acrshort{ILC can effectively compensate for potential inaccuracies in model parameter estimates by leveraging historical data.}
elements.sent-2411-006: Traffic congestion in modern cities presents a major challenge for reducing emissions and combating climate change.
elements.sent-2411-007: Despite the emergence of alternative transportation options, roads remain the primary mode of transport for both people and goods in the US and EU~\cite{eutransportfigures , with demand predicted to increase by 42\% by 2050~\cite{transport-in-eu .}}
elements.sent-2411-008: Beyond the environmental impact and the related health risks, congestion leads to significant economic losses due to inefficiencies, such as increased fuel consumption and delayed delivery of goods \cite{ferrara2018freeway .}
elements.sent-2411-009: Since naive solutions such as increasing capacity are not scalable, research has focused on optimizing the existing infrastructure.
elements.sent-2411-010: This approach requires a smaller investment and is adaptable to changing traffic conditions.
elements.sent-2411-011: The optimal operation of highway networks has long been a focus of research due to its significant impact on overall traffic.
elements.sent-2411-012: Numerous strategies have been proposed to reduce congestion \cite{papageorgiou2003review,papamichail2007motorway , with on-ramp metering management emerging as one of the most effective ones \cite{papageorgiou2002freeway, vrbanic2021variable .}}
elements.sent-2411-013: While highway on-ramp management has been well-studied, the role of \glspl{ST and their influence on traffic has gained attention only recently.}
elements.sent-2411-014: In \cite{cenedese2022novel,cenedese2023optimal , the authors proposed the \gls{CTM-s , showing that (if located strategically), a \gls{ST can reduce the peak traffic congestion on the highway stretch, acting as a damper preventing the congestion wave propagation.}}}
elements.sent-2411-015: In~\cite{kamalifar:2024:metanet_ST a second-order model based on METANET was proposed together with a simple control strategy based on ALINEA to regulate the flow of vehicles merging back into the mainstream from the \gls{ST .}}
elements.sent-2411-016: These previous works did not consider the repetitive nature of the traffic demand, which makes the problem inherently well-suited for learning-based control strategies such as \gls{ILC .}
elements.sent-2411-017: To tackle constraints systematically, the \gls{OB-ILC was then developed \cite{mishra2010optimization,owens2005iterative , with theoretical results including convergence analyses shown in \cite{amann1996iterative , \cite{schollig2009optimization .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-018: These results do not consider noise or model mismatch, both crucial features of real-world applications.
elements.sent-2411-019: Adaptive ILC methods are developed to deal with parametric uncertainties in discrete-time repetitive systems \cite{yu2016data,oh2015stochastic .}
elements.sent-2411-020: In \cite{nemec2017enhancing , adaptation is achieved by combining \gls{ILC with Reinforcement Learning.}}
elements.sent-2411-021: However, none of these methods provides robust constraint satisfaction guarantees.
elements.sent-2411-022: Recent work in \cite{liao2022robustness develops an \gls{OB-ILC scheme that uses a robust operator-theoretic framework to provide constraint satisfaction in the presence of noise and modeling errors for linear systems, also studied for iteration-varying systems~\cite{balta2024iterative .}}}
elements.sent-2411-023: The main contributions of this paper are: (i) The first \gls{MPC based ramp metering scheme using \gls{CTM-s to control the flow exiting a \gls{ST to reduce the mainstream congestion; (ii) a novel \gls{ILC scheme for highway traffic control, where we systematically integrate previous traffic data into the controller design to improve performance.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-024: Our approach relaxes the knowledge and modeling requirements by providing means to compensate for parameter estimation errors.
elements.sent-2411-025: The efficacy of our \gls{ILC scheme is validated via numerical studies demonstrating convergence to the correct parameter values and performance improvement.}
elements.sent-2411-026: In the model, time is discretized into intervals $[kT, (k+1)T)$ of length $T$ indexed by $k\in \mathbb{N}$, and the highway stretch is divided into $N$ consecutive cells indexed by $i \in \mathcal{N = \{0, ..., N-1\}$, see~\cref{fig:ctms .}}
elements.sent-2411-027: The vehicles exiting cell $i$ during the interval $k$ can either enter the subsequent cell $i+1$ or exit using an off-ramp if available.
elements.sent-2411-028: Similarly, new vehicles can enter cell $i$ from either cell $i-1$ or an on-ramp.
elements.sent-2411-029: The \gls{CTM-s exploits the presence of on and off ramps to model the presence of a \gls{ST .}}
elements.sent-2411-030: For simplicity, we assume there is only a single \gls{ST identified by the pair $(\ell,j)$, where $\ell,j\in\mc N$, with $\ell$ and $j$ denoting the exit and merge cells to the \gls{ST , respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-031: Differently from \cite{cenedese2022novel , we model separately the vehicles actively using the service station $l(k)$ and those waiting in a queue while merging back from the \gls{ST , $e(k)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-032: Moreover, we consider a highway stretch with no other ramps.
elements.sent-2411-033: It is important to emphasize that the following methodology can also be applied to more general cases, at the expense of more convoluted notation.
elements.sent-2411-034: Please refer to \cite{cenedese2022novel for an in-depth discussion of the model and possible generalizations that relax these assumptions.}
elements.sent-2411-035: Next, we report the dynamics of the model using the variables in~\cref{tab:ctms .}
elements.sent-2411-036: We divide the dynamics into linear and non-linear, which will be useful for controller design.
elements.sent-2411-037: The following dynamics guarantee the conservation of the number of vehicles and represent the cornerstone of the model.
elements.sent-2411-038: The density in cell $i$ evolves as \begin{equation}\rho_i(k+1) = \rho_i(k)+\tfrac{T}{L_i}\left(\Phi_i^+(k)-\Phi_i^-(k)\right), \label{eq:ctms_dyn_rho}\end{equation} where the total inflow and outflow are \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\Phi_i^+(k) = \phi_i(k)+r_i(k), \label{eq:ctms_dyn_Phi+} \Phi_i^-(k) = \phi_{i+1}(k)+s_i(k). \label{eq:ctms_dyn_Phi-}\end{equation} \end{subequations Notice that $s_i(k)=s(k)= \beta \Phi_i^-(k-1)$ if $i=\ell$ and $0$ otherwise, similarly $r_i(k)=r(k)$ only if $i=j$ and $0$ otherwise.}}
elements.sent-2411-039: Vehicles spend on average $\delta$ time intervals ($\delta\; T$) receiving service within the station before joining the queue to exit, thus \begin{subequations \begin{equation}l(k+1) = l(k)+T(s(k) - \phi_{l, e}(k)), \label{eq:ctms_dyn_l} e(k+1) = e(k)+T(\phi_{l,e}(k)-r(k)), \label{eq:ctms_dyn_e} \phi_{l, e}(k) = s(k-\delta). \label{eq:ctms_hist}\end{equation} \end{subequations.}}
elements.formula-2411-003: \begin{equation}l(k+1) = l(k)+T(s(k) - \phi_{l, e}(k)), \label{eq:ctms_dyn_l} e(k+1) = e(k)+T(\phi_{l,e}(k)-r(k)), \label{eq:ctms_dyn_e} \phi_{l, e}(k) = s(k-\delta). \label{eq:ctms_hist}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-040: The nonlinearities in the model are due to the flows among cells defined by the minimum between the upstream demand and downstream supply.
elements.sent-2411-041: The demand and supply for the cells and $\gls{ST}$ are defined as \begin{subequations \begin{equation}D_i(k) = \min((1-\beta_i) v_i\rho_i(k), q_i^max), \label{eq:D_i} S_i(k) = \min(w_i(\rho_i^max - \rho_i(k)), q_i^max), \label{eq:S_i} D^s(k) = \min\left(\phi_{l, e}(k)+\tfrac{e(k)}{T}, r^max\right), \label{eq:D_s}\end{equation} \end{subequations where in \eqref{eq:D_i , $\beta_i = \beta$ if $i = \ell$ and $\beta_{i = 0$ otherwise.}}}}
elements.formula-2411-004: \begin{equation}D_i(k) = \min((1-\beta_i) v_i\rho_i(k), q_i^max), \label{eq:D_i} S_i(k) = \min(w_i(\rho_i^max - \rho_i(k)), q_i^max), \label{eq:S_i} D^s(k) = \min\left(\phi_{l, e}(k)+\tfrac{e(k)}{T}, r^max\right), \label{eq:D_s}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-042: For all $i\neq j$, the flow between cell $i-1$ and $i$ reads as \begin{equation}\phi_i(k)= \min(D_{i-1}(k), S_i(k)). \label{eq:simple_merge}\end{equation} On the contrary, if $i=j$, the supply of cell $i$ is shared by \gls{ST and cell $i-1$, so \begin{equation}\phi_i(k) = \min (D_{i-1}(k), S^{\text{ms}}(k)), \label{eq:station_merge_phi}\end{equation} where $S^{\text{ms (k) \coloneqq \max (S_i(k)-D^s(k)), p^{\text{ms S_i(k))$, this formulation replaces the use of the middle operator, used in~\cite[Eq.~3.34]{ferrara2018freeway .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-043: Similarly, we can compute $r(k)$ as follows \begin{equation}r(k) = \min (D^s(k), S^{ST}(k)), \label{eq:station_merge_r}\end{equation} where $S^{\text{ST (k) \coloneqq \max (S_{j (k) - \phi_{j (k), (1-p^{\text{ms )S_{j (k))$ denotes the remaining supply of cell $j$ available to the vehicles that aim to merge back.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-044: As boundary conditions, we impose that $D_{-1 (k)$ is the exogenous upstream demand and that $S_N(k) = +\infty$, hence no downstream bottleneck influences the flow on the considered highway stretch.. \renewcommand*{\arraystretch {1.25.}}}
elements.figure-2411-002: \begin{center} \begin{tabular}{p{1.5cm}|c c l} Name Symbol Unit Description \multirow{5}{1.5cm}{Cell parameters} $L_i$ [km] Cell length $v_i$ [km/h] Free-flow speed $w_i$ [km/h] Congestion wave speed $q_i^\text{max}$ [veh/h] Flow capacity $\rho_i^\text{max}$ [veh/km] Jam density \cmidrule(lr){1-4} \multirow{6}{1.5cm}{\gls{ST} parameters} $l^\text{max}$ [veh] Service station capacity $e^\text{max}$ [veh] Queue length capacity $r^\text{max}$ [veh/h] On-ramp capacity $\delta \ T$ Average time spent at \gls{ST} $\beta$ [-] Split ratio at cell $\ell$ $p^\text{ms}$ [-] Priority of mainstream \cmidrule(lr){1-4} \multirow{6}{1.5cm}{Cell variables} $\rho_i(k)$ [veh/km] Density of cell $i$ $\Phi_i^+(k)$ [veh/h] Total inflow into cell $i$ $\Phi_i^-(k)$ [veh/h] Total outflow from cell $i$ $\phi_i(k)$ [veh/h] Flow from cell $i-1$ to $i$ $D_i(k)$ [veh/h] Demand of cell $i$ $S_i(k)$ [veh/h] Supply of cell $i$ \cmidrule(lr){1-4} \multirow{6}{1.25cm}{\gls{ST} variables} $l(k)$ [veh] Number of vehicles in \gls{ST} $e(k)$ [veh] Queue length $s(k)$ [veh/h] Inflow to \gls{ST} $\phi_{l,e}(k)$ [veh/h] Flow from $l(k)$ to $e(k)$ $D^s(k)$ [veh/h] Demand of \gls{ST} $r(k)$ [veh/h] On-ramp flow from \gls{ST} \cmidrule(lr){1-4} \multirow{2}{1.5cm}{Boundary conditions} $D_{-1}(k)$ [veh/h] Initial upstream demand $S_N(k)$ [veh/h] Downstream supply \end{tabular} \end{center} \caption{\gls{CTM-s} parameters and variables} \label{tab:ctms}
elements.sent-2411-045: First, we formulate an \gls{MPC that uses the \gls{CTM-s to optimally restrict the \gls{ST 's outflow to reduce the overall traffic congestion.}}}
elements.sent-2411-046: Then, we augment this with an \gls{ILC scheme, where we leverage the repetitiveness of the traffic demand.}
elements.sent-2411-047: In the case of \gls{ST , the \gls{MPC controller directly influences $D^s(k)$ via the input $r_c(k)$ that controls the maximum number of vehicles that can exit the \gls{ST during the interval $k$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-048: Therefore, the \gls{ST demand in \eqref{eq:D_s when the control is active becomes \begin{equation}D^s(k) = \min\left(\phi_{l, e}(k)+\tfrac{e(k)}{T}, r^max, r_c(k)\right). \label{eq:D_s controlled}\end{equation} The standard \gls{MPC formulation requires defining a cost function $J$ to be minimized during the current time interval $k_0$, over a prediction horizon of length $K$, subject to constraints associated with the model dynamics and inputs.}}}
elements.sent-2411-049: We consider a linear \gls{MPC for efficient computation and approximate the nonlinear \gls{CTM-s dynamics to attain only linear constraints.}}
elements.sent-2411-050: The nonlinear part of the \gls{CTM-s dynamics arises from \eqref{eq:demand supply --\eqref{eq:D_s controlled .}}}
elements.sent-2411-051: Inspired by \cite{gomes2006optimal , we linearize these equations by relaxation.}
elements.sent-2411-052: For all $i\neq j$, we relax \eqref{eq:simple_merge as \begin{equation}\phi_i(k) \leq \min (D_{i-1}(k), S_i(k)), \label{eq:relax_simple merge}\end{equation} where the two are equivalent if \eqref{eq:relax_simple merge is active.}}
elements.sent-2411-053: Substituting \eqref{eq:D_i and \eqref{eq:S_i into \eqref{eq:relax_simple merge leads to \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\phi_i(k) \leq (1-\beta_{i-1})v_{i-1}\rho_{i-1}(k), \phi_i(k) \leq q_{i-1}^max, \phi_i(k) \leq w_i(\rho_i^max - \rho_i(k)), \phi_i(k) \leq q_i^max.\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-054: Finally, \eqref{ineq:sum1 and \eqref{ineq:sum2 indicate that the sum of the two is constrained by the supply of the next cell $i$.}}
elements.sent-2411-055: Notice the approximation above ignores $p^\text{ms}$, i.e., we only consider the first terms in both $S^{\text{ms (k)$ and $S^{\text{ST (k)$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-056: In practice, $p^\text{ms}$ is usually close to $1$ hence the mainstream flow has a higher priority than the flow from the \gls{ST .}
elements.sent-2411-057: We promote this behavior in our controller by weighting $\phi_i(k)$ more than $r(k)$ in the cost function design, as detailed in the next section.
elements.sent-2411-058: Among the controller's goals, we also include limiting the maximum queue length at the exit of the \gls{ST .}
elements.sent-2411-059: This can be imposed via the following constraint \begin{equation}e(k) \leq e^max, \label{ineq:emax}\end{equation} where $e^\text{max}$ is the fixed maximum queue length.
elements.sent-2411-060: The primary control objective is to minimize the traffic congestion on the highway stretch.
elements.sent-2411-061: As the congestion metric, we use the \gls{TTT over the whole prediction horizon, which is a widely used metric in highway control in the literature, e.g., see \cite[Ch.8]{ferrara2018freeway , and it is defined as \begin{equation}TTT(k_0,k_0+K)\coloneqq {\textstyle \sum_{k=k_0}^{k_0 + K} \sum_{i=0}^{N-1} \rho_i(k) L_i.} \label{eq:TTT}\end{equation} To compensate for the relaxed constraints, we design a cost function to push the variables against the constraint \eqref{ineq:simple merge - \eqref{ineq:station merge .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-062: This approach leads to optimal solutions that satisfy a subset of the constraints with equality, which, in turn, satisfy the original non-linear \gls{CTM-s dynamics.}
elements.sent-2411-063: To this end, we maximize the flows $\phi_i(k)$ and $r(k)$.
elements.sent-2411-064: Therefore, the cost $J$ minimized by the \gls{MPC becomes \begin{equation}TTT(k_0,k_0+K)-\lambda \sum_{k=k_0}^{k_0 + K - 1} \left(w_r r(k) + \sum_{i=0}^{N} \phi_i(k) L_{i-1} \right), \label{eq:linear cost}\end{equation} where $\lambda>0$ and small $w_r$ ($0<w_r<L_{j-1}$) gives priority to the mainstream flow $\phi_j(k)$ when merging.}
elements.sent-2411-065: The last term in \eqref{eq:linear cost is called Total Travel Distance (TTD), see \cite[Ch.8]{ferrara2018freeway .}}
elements.sent-2411-066: Since there is no cell $-1$, we set $L_{-1}$ to a predefined weight.
elements.sent-2411-067: Note that during the control horizon $[k_0 T, (k_0+K)T]$, the flows $\phi_i(k_0+K)$ and $r(k_0+K)$ have not taken place yet, since by definition, they are flows during $[(k_0+K)T, (k_0+K+1)T)$.
elements.sent-2411-068: Therefore, the flows are only summed up to $K_0+K-1$ instead of $k_0+K$.
elements.sent-2411-069: For the same reason, in the following section, we define $\phi_i(k)$ as part of the inputs in the \gls{MPC formulation, even though we only regulate $r(k)$, since there are no initial values for them to propagate the dynamics, see \cref{fig:states and inputs .}}
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{States_and_Inputs.pdf} \caption{Relations between CTM-s model and MPC formulation. The non-linear dynamics \eqref{eq:demand supply} - \eqref{eq:D_s controlled} are relaxed as linear constraints \eqref{ineq:simple merge}, \eqref{ineq:station merge} with an extra cost term TTD. Moreover, the variables in blue boxes are known at time step $k$, while those in green boxes are only known after $k+1$. Thus, they are defined as states and inputs, respectively.} \label{fig:states and inputs}
elements.sent-2411-070: To formulate the linear \gls{MPC , we first organize the \gls{CTM-s variables as states $x(k) \coloneqq [\rho_0(k), \hdots \rho_{N-1 (k), l(k), e(k)]^\top\in \mathbb{R ^{N+2}$, and inputs $u(k) \coloneqq [\phi_0(k), \hdots, \phi_N(k), r(k)]^\top\in \mathbb{R ^{N+2 $.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-071: Then the collection of the variables over the prediction horizon is denoted by $x_{k_0 \coloneqq\mathrm{col}\left(\{x(k_0+k)\}_{k=0 ^K\right)\in \mathbb{R ^{(N+2) \times (K+1)}$ and $u_{k_0 \coloneqq\mathrm{col}\left(\{u(k_0+k)\}_{k=0 ^{K-1}\right)\in \mathbb{R ^{(N+2) \times K $, ${\phi_{l, e, k_0 }\coloneqq\mathrm{col}\left(\{\phi_{l, e (k_0+k)\}_{k=0 ^{K-1}\right) \in \mathbb{R ^{K}$.}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-072: The \gls{MPC at time at time $k_0$ reads as \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\min_{u_{k_0}} J(u_{k_0}) =\tfrac{a}{2} \lVert x_{k_0} - x_r \rVert ^2_Q + c_x^\top x_{k_0} - \ c_u ^\top u_{k_0},\label{MPC obj lifted} s.t. \quad x_{k_0} = x_{init, k_0} + G u_{k_0} + G_h \ {\phi_{l,e, k_0}}, \label{MPC eq cons lifted} A_{x} x_{k_0} + A_u u_{k_0} \leq b_{k_0}, \label{MPC ineq cons lifted} x_{k_0} \geq 0, u_{k_0} \geq 0. \label{MPC nonnegative lifted}\end{equation} \end{subequations In the objective function \eqref{MPC obj lifted , the two linear cost terms correspond to \eqref{eq:linear cost .}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-014: \begin{equation}\min_{u_{k_0}} J(u_{k_0}) =\tfrac{a}{2} \lVert x_{k_0} - x_r \rVert ^2_Q + c_x^\top x_{k_0} - \ c_u ^\top u_{k_0},\label{MPC obj lifted} s.t. \quad x_{k_0} = x_{init, k_0} + G u_{k_0} + G_h \ {\phi_{l,e, k_0}}, \label{MPC eq cons lifted} A_{x} x_{k_0} + A_u u_{k_0} \leq b_{k_0}, \label{MPC ineq cons lifted} x_{k_0} \geq 0, u_{k_0} \geq 0. \label{MPC nonnegative lifted}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-073: Moreover, we augment the cost with an additional quadratic term to provide necessary gradient information for the \gls{ILC scheme devised in the next section.}
elements.sent-2411-074: However, this quadratic term should be designed carefully since it potentially diverges the optimal solution from satisfying the subset of relaxed constraints \eqref{ineq:simple merge and \eqref{ineq:station merge with equality.}}
elements.sent-2411-075: Since we are to minimize $x$, we set $x_r:=0$ in our implementation.
elements.sent-2411-076: We further define a diagonal $Q\succ 0$, where its diagonal elements associated with $\rho_i(k)$, $l(k)$, and $e(k)$ are set to $w_{\rho L_i/ \rho_i ^ \text{max}$, $w_l / l ^ \text{max}$, and $w_e / e^\text{max}$, with weights $w_{\rho ,\,w_l,\,w_e>0$ normalized by $\rho_i ^ \text{max}$, $l ^ \text{max}$, and $e^\text{max}$, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-077: This ensures that the quadratic terms behave approximately linearly to conserve the tightness of the optimal solution at the subset of relaxed constraints \eqref{ineq:simple merge and \eqref{ineq:station merge as much as possible.}}
elements.sent-2411-078: The predefined $a>0$ is then used to achieve a reasonable trade-off between the gradient information and inaccuracy induced by relaxation.
elements.sent-2411-079: In \eqref{MPC eq cons lifted , $x_{\text{init , k_0}\coloneqq\mathbb{1}\otimes x(k_0)$ with $\mathbb{1}$ as a vector of ones of the correct dimension.}}
elements.sent-2411-080: The constraints \eqref{eq:ctms_dyn_rho -- \eqref{eq:ctms_ST_dynamics , \eqref{ineq:simple merge -- \eqref{ineq:emax are included in compact form in \eqref{MPC eq cons lifted , \eqref{MPC ineq cons lifted and \eqref{MPC nonnegative lifted , where $G,G_h,A_x,A_u$ are matrices of the correct dimensions.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-081: In practice, the time sampling interval $T$ is a design choice, and highway parameters $L_i$, $v_i$, $w_i$, $q_i^\text{max}$, $r^\text{max}$ are constant and can usually be measured precisely.
elements.sent-2411-082: However, the split ratio $\beta$, average waiting time $\delta$, and future initial demand $D_{-1 (k)$ depend on the drivers and are difficult to estimate.}
elements.sent-2411-083: Therefore, \eqref{program: MPC lifted is solved by using the best estimates $\beta_{\mathrm{es }$, $\delta_{\mathrm{es }$, and $D_{-1, \mathrm{es (k)$ of the corresponding parameters, i.e., \eqref{MPC eq cons lifted and \eqref{MPC ineq cons lifted are substituted by \begin{subequations \begin{equation}x_{k_0} = x_{init, k_0} + M u_{k_0} + G_h \ {\phi_{l,e, k_0, es}}, \label{MPC eq cons lifted est} A_{x, es} x_{k_0} + A_u u_{k_0} \leq b_{es}, \label{MPC ineq cons lifted est}\end{equation} \end{subequations where $M$, $A_{x, \mathrm{es }$, $b_{\mathrm{es }$, and ${\phi_{l,e, k_0, \mathrm{es }$ are estimates of $G$, $A_x$, $b$, and ${\phi_{l,e, k_0 }$, respectively, based on estimates $\beta_{\mathrm{es }$, $\delta_{\mathrm{es }$, and $D_{-1, \mathrm{es (k)$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-015: \begin{equation}x_{k_0} = x_{init, k_0} + M u_{k_0} + G_h \ {\phi_{l,e, k_0, es}}, \label{MPC eq cons lifted est} A_{x, es} x_{k_0} + A_u u_{k_0} \leq b_{es}, \label{MPC ineq cons lifted est}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-084: After solving the MPC problem, we set the obtained optimal input $r^*(k)$ as the control input $r_c(k)$ in \eqref{eq:D_s controlled .}
elements.sent-2411-085: Typically, in an MPC setting, the control input is updated at every time step.
elements.sent-2411-086: However, empirical experiments suggest that increasing the update interval has minimal impact on control performance.
elements.sent-2411-087: In our implementation, we update the control input every $p$ steps to reduce the computation load.
elements.sent-2411-088: As we will see in simulations, poor estimates $\beta_{\mathrm{es }$, $\delta_{\mathrm{es }$, and $D_{-1, \mathrm{es (k)$ can significantly undermine performance.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-089: To improve performance over time, we exploit the approximately repetitive nature of the problem.
elements.sent-2411-090: Specifically, the traffic pattern on the highway repeats from day to day and from week to week, enabling us to ``learn'' and ``improve'' the estimates of the unknown parameters based on previous data.
elements.sent-2411-091: For simplicity, we assume that the traffic pattern repeats daily, though our approach can be easily adapted to any period.
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{ILC_scheme.pdf} \caption{General framework of applying \gls{ILC} to highway traffic. Yellow and blue boxes denote system $k_0$ and system $k_0+p$ (with horizon length $K$), respectively. We compute the control inputs for system $k_0$ on iteration $d$ by \eqref{modified ILC} using data of the same system on iteration $d-1$. Then we apply the control inputs until updated at time step $k_0+p$ for the next system.} \label{fig:ILC}
elements.sent-2411-092: We implement \gls{ILC in a receding-horizon fashion.}
elements.sent-2411-093: For computing control actions at time step $k_0$ of iteration $d$, i.e., the $d$-th day, we use the data of time steps $k \in [k_0, k_0+K]$ collected during the previous iteration $d-1$.
elements.sent-2411-094: We update control inputs every $p$ time step.
elements.sent-2411-095: Therefore, at time step $k_0+p$, the control inputs are recomputed using the data of $k \in [k_0+p, k_0+p+K]$ of the previous iteration.
elements.sent-2411-096: This process repeats until the entire desired control period is covered.
elements.sent-2411-097: We call the traffic between $k_0$ and $k_0+K$ (denoted by the yellow boxes in~\cref{fig:ILC ) system $k_0$, which corresponds to the nominal problem \eqref{program: MPC lifted at $k_0$.}}
elements.sent-2411-098: Similarly, we call the traffic between $k_0+p$ and $k_0+p+K$ (denoted by blue boxes in~\cref{fig:ILC ) system $k_0 + p$, and so on.}
elements.sent-2411-099: Note that we iterate horizontally rather than vertically.
elements.sent-2411-100: This is because the repetitive pattern of highway traffic occurs simultaneously on each iteration (e.g., day), while the traffic during a single iteration (e.g., during one day) can vary largely at different time steps.
elements.sent-2411-101: For example, the traffic demand during the morning peak strongly differs from one in the evening, see~\cref{fig:initial demand , on the contrary, the morning peak traffic demand on one day is usually similar to one of the previous day.}
elements.sent-2411-102: We propose an \gls{ILC formulation based on~\cite{liao2022robustness and focus on a particular system $k_0$ and omit the subscript ``$k_0$'' if there is no ambiguity.}}
elements.sent-2411-103: The subscript $d$ denotes the iteration index.
elements.sent-2411-104: Based on \eqref{program: MPC lifted , we propose the following \gls{ILC formulation.}}

elements.sent-2411-106: For the objective \eqref{eq:ILC obj , $W := M^\top Q M \succ 0$ is a preconditioner matrix.}
elements.sent-2411-107: Note that in our formulation, $M$ has full column rank, therefore it is guaranteed that $W \succ 0$ as long as $Q \succ 0$.
elements.sent-2411-108: For iteration $d$, by replacing \eqref{MPC obj lifted into \eqref{MPC eq cons lifted , we attain \begin{equation}J_d(u) = \frac{1}{2} u^\top H u + f_d^\top u + c,\end{equation} where $H := a \; G ^\top Q G$, $f_d:=a \; G^\top Q (w_d - x_r) + G^\top c_x - c_u$, $w_d:=G_h{\phi_{l, e _d + x_{\text{init,d }$, and $c:=G^\top c_x - c_u$.}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-016: \begin{equation}J_d(u) = \frac{1}{2} u^\top H u + f_d^\top u + c,\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-017: \begin{equation}\nabla J_d(u) = H u + f_d \nonumber = a\; [G^\top Q(Gu+w_d-x_r)] + G^\top c_x - c_u. \label{eq:gradient}\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-018: \begin{equation}F(x_{d-1}, u_{d-1})\! :=\! a\;[M^\top Q(x_{d-1}-x_r)]\! +\! M^\top c_x\! -\! c_u \label{eq:gradient_approx}\end{equation}

elements.sent-2411-110: This is equivalent to replacing $w_d$ by $w_{d-1}$, since $x_{d-1 = G u_{d-1 + w_{d-1}$, according to \eqref{MPC eq cons lifted .}}}

elements.sent-2411-114: After computing \eqref{modified ILC , we apply the optimal solution $r^*(k)$ for $p$ time intervals as $r_c(k)$, and then compute a new control input sequence by solving a new instance of \eqref{modified ILC associated with the next system.}}
elements.sent-2411-115: We consider a highway stretch identified by the parameters in~\cref{tab:simparams .}

elements.sent-2411-119: The upstream demand $D_{-1 (k)$ is as in~\cref{fig:initial demand that is a scaled version of the flow along the A2 highway in the Netherlands during a standard weekday, extracted from the Nationaal Dataportaal Wegverkeer \cite{ndw_traffic_data .}}}
elements.sent-2411-120: We focus only on the morning peak from $07:00$ to $10:00$, the initial and final time of the considered peak are denoted by $t_{\rm s}$ and $t_{\rm e}$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-121: We model the error in the initial estimates of $\beta$, $\delta$, and $D_{-1 (k)$, by the scaling factors $r_\beta,\,r_\delta,\,r_D\geq0$.}
elements.sent-2411-122: The estimated parameters are \begin{equation}\beta_{es}=r_\beta\beta,\quad \delta_{es}=r_\delta\delta,\quad D_{-1, es}(k)=r_D D_{-1}(k), \label{eq:estimates}\end{equation} where a scaling greater or smaller than $1$ denotes an over- or underestimation of the corresponding parameter.

elements.sent-2411-125: Notice that, during the first day ($d=0$), the \gls{ILC scheme cannot be applied, as there is no prior day's data available.}
elements.sent-2411-126: Therefore, an \gls{MPC is used with parameters $\beta_{\mathrm{es }$, $ \delta_{\mathrm{es }$, and $ D_{-1,{\mathrm{es }$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-127: Since our primary goal is to reduce congestion, we use TTT$(t_{\rm s ,t_{\rm e )$ as main evaluation metric.}}
elements.sent-2411-128: The controller trad offs between alleviating congestion and maintaining a low waiting time at the \gls{ST exit.}
elements.sent-2411-129: Thus, we consider two additional metrics the Total Waiting Time (TWT) and the Total Time Spent (TTS), defined as \begin{subequations \begin{equation}TWT(t_{\rm s},t_{\rm e}) \coloneqq \sum_{k=t_{\rm s}}^{t_{\rm e}} e(k), \label{eq:metricsTWT} TTS(t_{\rm s},t_{\rm e}) \coloneqq TTT(t_{\rm s},t_{\rm e}) + TWT(t_{\rm s},t_{\rm e}). \label{eq:metricsTTS}\end{equation} \end{subequations TWT measures the waiting time for vehicles at the \gls{ST exit, while TTS represents the overall time for traveling and waiting.}}}
elements.formula-2411-022: \begin{equation}TWT(t_{\rm s},t_{\rm e}) \coloneqq \sum_{k=t_{\rm s}}^{t_{\rm e}} e(k), \label{eq:metricsTWT} TTS(t_{\rm s},t_{\rm e}) \coloneqq TTT(t_{\rm s},t_{\rm e}) + TWT(t_{\rm s},t_{\rm e}). \label{eq:metricsTTS}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-130: Moreover, to better assess the convergence performance of \gls{ILC to \gls{GT-MPC , we define the following relative indices \eblue \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\Delta_TTT \coloneqq TTT^{ILC}(t_{\rm s},t_{\rm e})- TTT^{GT-MPC}(t_{\rm s},t_{\rm e}),\label{eq:DeltaTTT} \Delta_TWT \coloneqq TWT^{ILC}(t_{\rm s},t_{\rm e})- TWT^{GT-MPC}(t_{\rm s},t_{\rm e}),\label{eq:DeltaTWT} \Delta_TTS \coloneqq TTS^{ILC}(t_{\rm s},t_{\rm e})- TTS^{GT-MPC}(t_{\rm s},t_{\rm e}),\label{eq:DeltaTTS}\end{equation} \end{subequations where the superscript indicates the controller used.}}}}
elements.formula-2411-023: \begin{equation}\Delta_TTT \coloneqq TTT^{ILC}(t_{\rm s},t_{\rm e})- TTT^{GT-MPC}(t_{\rm s},t_{\rm e}),\label{eq:DeltaTTT} \Delta_TWT \coloneqq TWT^{ILC}(t_{\rm s},t_{\rm e})- TWT^{GT-MPC}(t_{\rm s},t_{\rm e}),\label{eq:DeltaTWT} \Delta_TTS \coloneqq TTS^{ILC}(t_{\rm s},t_{\rm e})- TTS^{GT-MPC}(t_{\rm s},t_{\rm e}),\label{eq:DeltaTTS}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-131: Finally, we define the maximum rate of violating the queue length constraint \eqref{ineq:emax over $[t_s, t_e]$ as \begin{equation}\Delta_{e^{max}}(t_{\rm s}, t_{\rm e}) \coloneqq \max_{k \in [t_{\rm s}, t_{\rm e}]} \left( \frac{\max\left(e(k) - e^{max}, 0\right)}{e^{max}} \right). \label{eq:metricsvio}\end{equation} If there is never a violation, then $\Delta_{e^{\text{max =0$.. \renewcommand*{\arraystretch {1.25.}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-024: \begin{equation}\Delta_{e^{max}}(t_{\rm s}, t_{\rm e}) \coloneqq \max_{k \in [t_{\rm s}, t_{\rm e}]} \left( \frac{\max\left(e(k) - e^{max}, 0\right)}{e^{max}} \right). \label{eq:metricsvio}\end{equation}

elements.sent-2411-132: Notice that TTS are almost equal in both cases, indicating the trade-off between TTT and TTS, as expected.
elements.sent-2411-133: Thanks to its precise knowledge of the parameters, \gls{GT-MPC is effective, as it reduces congestion (attain lower TTT) while maintaining $\Delta_{e^{\text{max = 0$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-134: Note that the first three sub-figures are presented on a logarithmic scale.
elements.sent-2411-135: At iteration $0$, since \gls{MPC with estimated parameters is applied, the positive values of $\Delta_\mathrm{TTT}$ suggest that the control effect of reducing congestion can be undermined by imprecise knowledge of the parameters.}
elements.sent-2411-136: In some cases (e.g., underestimating $\beta$), due to wrong estimates, the controller can result in both positive $\Delta_\mathrm{TTT}$ and $\Delta_\mathrm{TWT}$ simultaneously, leading to greater overall TTS.
elements.sent-2411-137: Moreover, the queue length constraints can be violated as well.
elements.sent-2411-138: Remarkably, by exploiting previous data at subsequent steps, \gls{ILC is able to converge rapidly towards \gls{GT-MPC (within one or two iterations) in all cases, achieving lower $\Delta_\mathrm{TTT}$ (therefore lower TTT), as indicated by the fast convergence of all curves to a neighborhood of $0$ value.}}
elements.sent-2411-139: Moreover, the queue length constraint can always be satisfied.
elements.sent-2411-140: This shows the effectiveness of our \gls{ILC approach.}
elements.sent-2411-141: Numerical experiments show that by leveraging the repetitive traffic pattern, our \gls{ILC approach converges to the optimal \gls{MPC controller with ground-truth parameters.}}
elements.sent-2411-142: Our \gls{ILC method recovers from the incorrect initial estimations in a few iterations and outperforms \gls{MPC under the same conditions, remarkably, even when the parameter values differ by $20\%$ from the correct ones.}}
elements.sent-2411-143: This study focuses on a single highway with one service station.
elements.sent-2411-144: An interesting extension is to use this approach to control more complex road networks with multiple stations, where coordinated control may yield better performance.
elements.sent-2411-145: Another potential direction is developing a nonlinear \gls{ILC to address model mismatch introduced by the linear relaxation of the \gls{CTM-s model, which inherently contains nonlinear dynamics.}}
elements.sent-2411-146: Our current formulation requires an extra cost term TTD, which can potentially diverge the controller from the original goal of reducing TTT.
elements.sent-2411-147: With non-linear formulation, we might skip relaxation and remove TTD, and therefore reduce congestion more effectively.. \addtolength{\textheight {-12cm \bibliographystyle{ieeetr.}}}
